Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered most of the time. I tried to set aside time in the evenings after my part-time work, but with limited resources at home, I often had to study whenever I could get reliable internet access. I'd usually batch my studying into longer sessions on weekends when the library was open and I could use their computers. It wasn't ideal, but I made it work with what I had.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due. When deadlines were approaching, I'd push myself harder and study late into the night. Other weeks when things were calmer, I'd try to maintain about 15 hours spread across the week, but life kept throwing curveballs at me. Between work and family responsibilities, regular schedules felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or seeing my peers talking about an upcoming assignment that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd get anxious just thinking about falling behind, and that fear would push me to check the LMS even when I wasn't sure what I was looking for. Notifications from my study group also prompted me to log in because I didn't want to miss out on helpful discussions.
Q4: After a few weeks, checking the LMS became almost automatic, especially in the mornings before I started my day. The peer support group I was connected with made it feel less like a chore and more like checking in with people who understood my struggles. When my classmates started posting regularly in discussions, I felt obligated to keep up, and that routine stuck with me throughout the course.
Q5: The video lectures were really helpful for me because I'm a visual learner and I could pause and rewatch sections I didn't understand. I also found the peer interactions incredibly valuable because other students would explain concepts in simpler terms than the textbook. The discussion forums let me see that I wasn't alone in struggling with certain topics, which honestly kept me going when I felt like giving up.
Q6: I often skipped the supplementary readings because they took too long to get through with my limited internet data. Some of the optional quizzes I delayed because they felt overwhelming when I was already anxious about the main assignments. I knew they could help, but when you're scared about failing the important stuff, the extra materials feel like too much to handle.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to passing the course or when it helped reduce my anxiety about upcoming exams. If my peers recommended something or if it was discussed in our support group, I'd prioritize it. Basically, if I could see how it would help me survive the course, I'd make time for it even when things were tight.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a risk to me because time spent on them was time not spent on required work. With required activities, there was no question—I had to do them or fail. Optional ones made me anxious because I'd wonder if I was making the right choice by skipping them. Sometimes I'd feel guilty about not doing them, but I had to be strategic with my limited time and energy.
Q9: Yes, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs when I had good internet access, then study them at home without being connected. I also borrowed textbooks from classmates and took handwritten notes that I'd review on the bus to work. My LMS activity probably looked minimal during those times, but I was actually putting in real effort.
Q10: There were many times I'd print materials at the library and study them for hours at home without any digital trace. I'd also form small study groups with classmates where we'd meet in person and work through problems together. Those sessions were some of my most productive learning moments, but the LMS wouldn't know anything about them. My peer interactions online were just the tip of the iceberg.
Q11: Definitely. I'd often watch a video once, take detailed notes, and then review those notes multiple times offline. With my data limitations, I couldn't afford to stream videos repeatedly, so I had to be efficient. I'd sometimes download materials at school and study them over several days at home. One viewing on the LMS didn't mean I only engaged with that content once.
Q12: There were weeks when my anxiety got really bad, and I'd feel paralyzed just looking at the course materials. When multiple assignments piled up at once, I felt scared and overwhelmed, like I was drowning. Financial stress at home also made it hard to focus because I was worried about things beyond school. During those times, even logging in felt like facing something terrifying.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from work or dealing with family issues, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules altogether because I was scared of feeling stupid. The fatigue was real—sometimes I'd fall asleep trying to watch lectures after a long shift at work.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd be thinking about bills or worrying about whether I'd pass, and nothing was actually sinking in. It looked like engagement on paper, but mentally I was checked out. Those were frustrating moments because I knew I was wasting precious study time.
Q15: I postponed working on assignments that felt too difficult because I was scared of confirming that I couldn't do it. The fear of failure made me avoid things even when I knew procrastinating would make it worse. Sometimes I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt more ready, but that readiness never really came. It was a cycle driven by anxiety more than laziness.
Q16: Deadlines were like alarm bells that forced me into action. I'd increase my LMS activity dramatically in the days before something was due, cramming and panicking. Without deadlines, I might have drifted and not engaged at all, so in a way they kept me accountable. But they also spiked my anxiety to uncomfortable levels, making the whole experience stressful.
Q17: When I got positive feedback, it motivated me to keep going and made me feel like maybe I could actually do this. Negative or critical feedback sometimes made me want to hide and avoid the course for a few days. The peer support helped me process feedback better because my classmates would encourage me not to give up. Feedback definitely influenced my emotional state and how quickly I'd return to the LMS.
Q18: The course structure with clear deadlines helped me stay on track even when I was struggling. Having modules broken into smaller pieces made it less overwhelming than one massive chunk of content. But when multiple things were due at once, the structure felt suffocating rather than helpful. I appreciated when the course gave some flexibility because my life didn't always align with rigid schedules.
Q19: The video content being available on-demand made engagement easier because I could study when I actually had time and internet access. However, some of the assignment instructions were confusing, which made me anxious and hesitant to start. The peer discussion features were great because they connected me with others in similar situations. Clearer guidelines would have reduced a lot of my fear around assignments.
Q20: I think students might be searching for help or trying to understand what's expected without feeling confident enough to submit work. They could be scared of submitting something wrong, like I often was. High activity might mean they're engaged mentally but paralyzed when it comes to actually producing something. Fear of judgment or failure can create that gap between browsing and submitting.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience in some ways. I did a lot of learning offline that never showed up in the system, and my exam score was decent despite not having high task completion rates. Some students are efficient—they get what they need quickly and study elsewhere. The LMS activity doesn't capture the full picture of how hard someone is actually working.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and what you click on, which gives some insight into habits. But they miss the quality of engagement—whether you actually understood something or just scrolled past it. They can't capture offline studying, emotional state, or the support you get from peers outside the platform. The numbers tell a partial story at best.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material, not just going through the motions to pass. It's showing up mentally even when you're scared or tired, and pushing through the anxiety because you want to succeed. For me, engagement also meant connecting with peers who kept me accountable and supported. It's a commitment that goes beyond just clicking buttons on a website.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I was most of the time, or how much courage it took just to keep going. It wouldn't capture the late-night study sessions with borrowed textbooks or the encouragement I got from my peer support group in person. My low resources and the sacrifices I made to stay in this course are invisible in the data. The resilience it took to pass despite everything—that's not something any log can measure.
